sentences.text: In 1939, when the Germans came to Poland, and they came into Staszow, it was very, very, very bad.
We-- the family was still together.
My brothers, two brothers--
Kalmun, ?] and [?
Hosker, ?] and [?
Revcha, ?] and my father, and my other sister, [?
Marma, ?] which, the same sister-- the same father and mother.
So we're still together.
The war broke out.
And the Germans were there, in Poland.
In 1939, this was-- they came in October sometimes.
And I remember, in December-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in December-- so, you know, it started to be very bad.
You know, we couldn't work anymore.
Because they started to harass the Jewish merchants.
Them-- the-- the people, which we were working for them, as contractors-- they-- they went into that-- the minute they came into Poland-- the Germans-- they went into that store.
They vandalized the whole store.
They took out everything what they had there.
And they gave it away to the Poles.
And they we're grabbing everything, those Poles.
You know, they were so happy.
And-- and when they've seen a Jewish man, with a beard, and things like that-- oh, Jew.
You, Jew.
[POLISH].
And [POLISH], [POLISH], [POLISH].
And-- and they called, right away, the Germans, you know, the SS.
And they started to harass, and to beat, and to shoot, and to do everything.
It was unbearable [INAUDIBLE].
With what?
Oh, sure.
I-- now I witnessed everything.
Because I was a youngster at that time-- you know, 13 years old.
I wanted to know what's going on.
So I-- and I was blond, you know.
If somebody didn't know me, I could go on as a Pole.
You know, they didn't know that I'm even Jewish.
I was blond.
I have a blond hair-- you know, things like that.
Yeah, I witnessed they came in.
The minute they came in one store, I went out.
This was like a-- the merchant, you know, that the city was like a round city-- four streets.
And in the middle was like a marketplace.
And I witnessed one time, they went into a Jewish store, a textile store.
And they-- the stores were closed at that time.
Because the Jews were afraid right away.
So they broke over-- open this store.
And they went into the textile store.
They was very rich people, you know.
They-- they were a lot of money, those people.
And they broke open the store.
And they started to take.
And the Poles were grabbing, went all over.
And the SS were starting to throw out all the merchandise.
They emptied out the store in ten minutes.
And I witnessed the way they were fighting to grab a piece of material [INAUDIBLE].
They were throwing it out, like that, and things like that.
So this was the first thing they came in.
And they made from all the Jewish merchants which they had stores and things like that, right away.
They liquidate.
They killed They-- you know, I was going in my street, when I was going by.
And the first thing, at night, they were taking out people from the houses.
And they told them, you know, like one went by an SS.
And they told them, you're going to guard this street that nobody should come in it.
If you see something, you should report to the SS.
So, they took out the guy.
And-- and-- and he was going around there, and you know.
And they told him, if not, we're going to shoot you.
So about a half an hour later, the same SS man came by.
And they've seen this guy there walking.
He took out the gun.
They shot him right in the-- right there, without any questions, without-- you told me-- you told him to go around there.
So he shot him a half an hour later.
I remember another thing, when they were-- they were staying in Staszow.
There was like-- before the war, there was a Polish army there, the barracks.
And the Polish army wasn't there.
So they took over.
The SS took it over.
So they grabbed-- needed something to be done there, in those barracks.
And they were going in the street and gathering, you know, like workers, like Jewish-- Jewish-- just Jewish men, whoever they've seen.
Come on, come on, come on.
They took us.
They get it together, maybe-- and I wasn't between them so [? help. ?] I was-- in the same time, I was around there.
So they grabbed me.
And they took us to those barracks.
And they divided everybody to gave some chores, you know, to-- you do this.
You do this.
You clean this.
You clean the toilets.
You clean the barracks, and everything.
So me, as a youngster-- I was the youngest ones there.
They grabbed-- I don't know from how this happened, even.
I still can remember.
They gave me-- there was a pile with sand, you know, outside the barracks.
So they gave me a shovel.
And they said, take this sand from here, and put it there.
Because, you know, they were talking to us in German, not Polish-- German.
But Jewish and German is similar.
I can understand when a German is contacting me.
I can understand everything.
So, I took the shovel.
And I started to shift this sand from here, to here, to here-- back and forth, back and forth.
So it didn't go by maybe a half an hour.
So I-- this whole stack of-- of sand was shifted over there.
So an SS man comes over to me.
What did you do?
[SPEAKING GERMAN]?
I said, you told me to do it.
In Jewish I spoke to him.
He understand very well.
You told me to do it from here, to put it over there.
And he gave me a lick.
And I came home.
I was so bleeding from all over-- bleeding from all over.
And then, they let us go, again, after that.
I said, you told me to do it.
And I did it.
It didn't help.
[NON-ENGLISH].
Yes.
She was a very sick.
She was sick.
She was in bed, [INAUDIBLE] bedridden.
And I don't think that she went out too much.
But she know what's going on, and things like that.
She knew everything.
She was sick.
And she died.
And we buried her in Staszow.
I was-- lately, last year, I was in Staszow.
And I went to the cemetery.
But nothing was there left.
You know, they destroyed everything.
And kind of, I still remembered where they put her.
And I was looking for it-- no sign for the grave and [INAUDIBLE].
What could he feel?
He was very bitter.
He couldn't move.
He couldn't go.
He-- he didn't have no money to go to do something.
He was looking up to the kids, you know, the-- my older brothers.
The-- he was married, you know, the oldest brother, here, who was in the picture.
He was married.
And he was looking to him for handouts.
You know, he couldn't-- he didn't have no money.
He didn't have nothing.
So, what-- what could he have done?
Sitting at home and waiting-- what's going to happen?
What's going to happen?
So everybody was so disgusted when they came into Poland.
And they started.
And you heard-- first of all, they started to take everybody away, you know, to work camps.
I don't know.
I wasn't in that camp.
I was later.
In 1940 already, they came to Staszow.
And Staszow had a Judenrat, like they say.
You know, like a-- a congress, or something like that.
Every city had, you know, like a president, a Jewish president, only for Jews.
They chose, because they were involved before that.
They knew.
They came in, they have, right away, everything.
They came in there.
And they knew everything and everybody.
Yes.
Yes.
So they came in there.
In 1940, when I came in-- so they came in-- I was working at-- in Staszow, they had a [NON-ENGLISH] called [?
Omle. ?] They were building highways.
And I was working for this company, as a youngster.
We were gathering in the morning.
We were gathering about 50, 100 people together.
And they transported us.
We marched-- we didn't transport.
We marched to that-- to those sites, which we have to work.
And I was working there.
No, nothing was known-- anything.
They promised us-- when they took us away, they promised that we have to work.
They're going to pay us.
They give us food.
They give us accommodations and everything.
Nothing is out of the ordinary, you know?
Everything is fine.
So-- and the-- the Judenrat came out that everybody, from every family-- not from the rich families.
The rich families-- they gave money.
And they didn't touch them.
Because it was all bribes.
But they came to the average family.
So they came out.
And they took from-- from our family two person-- my brother [?
Hosker ?] and me they took out.
And there was 150.
They came with trucks.
They took us out to Skarzysko.
Absolutely nothing.
They told-- they didn't let us take even nothing, even clothing-- nothing.
You don't have to.
Just go in the truck and disappear.
Good bye.
That's about it.
We kissed.
We hugged-- you know, with the sisters.
We were crying, and things like that.
And so, oh-- we went away.
Disappeared.
That's about it.
And then, when we came into Skarzysko, it was entirely a different story what they told us what it's going to be.
And then the trouble started.
When we first came into Skarzysko, the SS was there in a [NON-ENGLISH], the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], which they were guarding, at the concentration camp.
There were Ukrainian-- mostly Ukrainian people, from Ukraine.
And right away, they put us to work.
And this was an ammunition factory.
They were producing there cannons-- big, big ones-- big ones like that, you know, with the big [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the big [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what they say.
They were producing this.
Me, they send-- you know, I was very young.
My brother went to work in the fields, building barracks again.
And me, they took-- I was going with my brother.
So they gave me to dig ditches, and things like that.
This was the first thing.
Food was in the morning.
[NON-ENGLISH] was about 5 o'clock in the morning.
And then, they gave you a little bit of coffee and one piece of bread-- you know, maybe five ounces or something like that.
And we went to work, 6 o'clock, every day.
And at noon hour, they gave us a soup.
And this was the whole thing.
The soup-- the soup was poison-- water, nothing else.
You know.
And then, when we came back to the barracks, they gave us another piece of bread.
This was the whole things what I-- we ate.
But we worked.
But what happened, when we came to Skarzysko-- so everybody, whoever had a few dollars-- you know, dollars or Polish money, or something like that.
And we were working with Pollacks together.
Some of them weren't bad.
You know, if you gave them a few dollars or something, if you gave them-- they [INAUDIBLE] in the morning, they brought us in as much as they could, you know.
[COUGHING]
They couldn't-- they couldn't bring in too much.
But they were hiding, you know.
And-- but if you paid them, they brought us in a little bit.
Some of them, the Poles, weren't bad.
Because-- at work, they helped as much as they could.
But some of them-- they were worse than the Germans.
They were killing more Jews in that concentration camp than the Germans did-- the Pollacks.
But this is-- this was the routine.
And you've seen-- in that-- in Skarzysko, there was three concentration camps.
One was called [NON-ENGLISH].
One was [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH] A, B, and C.
I was in the C.
The C was the ammunition factory.
The [NON-ENGLISH] A, which they were [? worked, ?] was about, maybe, a half an hour going, you know, by foot.
To go to that, there, was-- were much better.
Because they were-- were not too heavy works.
Because they were making easy work, you know-- but just machines and things like that.
And they gave them treatments better than in-- in our camp.
So-- but this was the worst one.
Every day that you have seen in that camp dying people, just like flies-- dying, just dying.
Because the work was so bad, in bad conditions.
When you were working-- I was working for a short while there, at those big ammunition [INAUDIBLE].
You could only last there maybe a month-- maybe a month, if you would have to be in good health and good strength.
You became yellow.
Worse than this-- the whole body was falling apart.
Whatever you were wearing was falling apart.
It ate you up alive.
When the Poles were working before, in those ammunition factories, before the war, they were working.
This was a Polish ammunition factory.
So they-- they had-- 10 minutes they were working, 10 minutes they were outside.
And they were wearing masks, and special uniform, and things like that.
With us, we were working 12 hours a day, and with nothing.
So you became yellow.
Anything you touched was bitter, like the worst thing in your life.
No matter what you ate, it was bitter.
So I was-- I was fortunate enough that on the-- on the-- on the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, like which they were running that camp, it was Jews from Chenstochov.
And I had there, which he paid off.
So a guy, which was working in a-- at home, at Staszow, with us to get it.
So he knew me.
So he took me out-- you know, [NON-ENGLISH], like the saying.
So took me out from that work.
And they put into a easier work.
So this was my only good luck, good fortune.
And then my brother, he was working also-- not too hard work.
And he got-- he got sick, in typhus.
He was laying in the barracks with who knows how much-- how high that fever was.
Maybe 100, 150, hundred-- nobody.
No doctors.
No doctor.
But he was-- he didn't know.
He was unconscious.
So the fever was so high, he was unconscious.
And one day-- like, was on a Sunday.
I went out to work, like at-- and when I came to work, I tended to him, and things like that.
I went out Sunday to work.
I came back.
They told me the news that they took him out.
Where did they took him?
Shot him.
Was-- that Sunday was Black Sunday, like they say.
And they took out about 100 people from those barracks.
Whoever was left over in the barracks, didn't go to work that time-- took him out.
Shot him.
This was then.
So I lost a brother.
I haven't got no brother.
So I all by myself-- 14 years old.
After-- what, the--
All-- all were friends.
All were in the same position.
Everybody was waiting.
Tomorrow you going to go.
There wasn't much to-- to cry about it.
Because you knew that the next day, or next week, you going to be next.
You going to be in the same position.
Every day, there was a-- 50, 100 people, dead.
So it was no-- no big deal.
More were dying.
More they were bringing in every-- every-- practically every week, every second week, they brought in a new transport, a new transport, you know, to work.
It was the hard work-- very hard work.
And where did the transports come from?
They were coming from all over Poland.
They came from Warsaw.
They came with a transport from-- from Krakow, from Chenstochov-- wherever they could get it, the Jews, young ones, to work, they brought them there-- transports from all over.
So this was-- this was the lifestyle there, in the concentration camp-- nothing to look forward.
And you knew what is next going to be waiting you-- for you.
So I was there till 19-- 1940 till 1944.
I don't know.
I told you, I-- I was transferred to a very easy work.
Like, you know, there was nothing, practically-- they didn't hurt me too much.
And as far as food was concerned, there they were giving better food-- like, a little bit more bread.
And beside that, I had there some people, which they had enough to eat.
So you know, they always gave you a piece of bread, or something like that.
So this was the whole thing.
Oh yeah, I was ill, also.
I was ill there, but not severely ill.
But I also had typhus.
But my typhus wasn't like that, that I was laying out.
I would have to lay down, you know, to-- but I was afraid.
What I knew, already, what they'd done to my brother.
So I was afraid to stay in the barracks.
Even when I couldn't go, even when I couldn't work anymore, I was going to work in the morning.
They took me, you know, those people, which we worked together.
And at work, they substitute for me.
And I lay down there.
I could have-- you know, in the side there.
They didn't come in to check so much.
Just Poles were checking.
And they knew what's going on.
So they let you go.

This was the-- you know, to-- to-- to tell you how this happens, and whether this is luck or it's supposed to be like that, or it's your destiny, like they say, you're supposed to survive-- you know, it's hard to explain.
No.
They couldn't.
Well, what-- what happen?
They didn't have-- even have a prayer in their life.
Right away, they were shooting.
They catch the guys-- you know, in that camp, in 1944, I remember distinctly.
When they-- this was-- short before they took us out from that camp.

Because the-- the war was going on closer and closer, you know? 1944, they were fighting with already in the territories of Poland, the territories-- you know, it was right, close before the war was over.
They catch the guy-- a father and three sons.
They catch them, that they were at from work, they got-- somehow, they got some bread, or potatoes, and things like that.
They catch them at the-- going into the barracks.
They catch them-- what do you got?
They searched them.
They took it out, everything, away from them.
They took them to the barracks.
They didn't let them go to their barracks.
They took them out in a special barrack.
Next day, they took out everybody from the barracks.
They make, like, an appeal.
You know, everybody have to go out, from the whole camp.
We lined up.
You know, and then they took out the four-- four guys, and they hanged them, right in the front of everybody.
This was just a routine, to scare the other ones, they shouldn't bring in anything, or they shouldn't try to escape, or something like that.
This was it.
Yes.
How did they do that?
Just-- they just-- they didn't have to do much.
They went over to the fences, close to the fences.
They shot them right away.
They had [INAUDIBLE].
This was it.
No.
I knew-- I knew that because they came in from-- some letters, through Poles.
So I knew that Staszow was you know, emptied, from Jews.
They took them-- everybody they took-- I think to Auschwitz, or to Treblinka, at that time.
So I knew.
And-- and I knew that my oldest brother-- they left that time in they-- the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, that was from Staszow.
They left.
In the shul, they made a working camp.
And they took together all the tailors, which they knew how to sew.
And they put up, like, in the shul, upstairs-- there was an upstairs.
They put up machines.
And they were working for the Germans.
Uniforms they were making.
They brought in materials and things like that.
And they were working there.
So they left a few hundred.
And my father-- the older brother took my father with him, which he couldn't do anything.
But since he was there, so he took him with him.
And they were there for a few months.
And then they took him out.
And they sent him away.
My father went-- that's what they told me.
I don't know exactly.
But that's what they told me.
They went to another city.
This was [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].
And he died there.
That's what they told me about it.
And that's as far as I know about.
And the rest of the family, like my oldest brother, with the family, he had three kids-- three girls.
With his wife, they took him.
They sent him away.
Never heard of him again.
We-- the minute they started to shuffle around, we heard, you know, through news, which the Poles brought into the camps, that they are here, they're fighting here, they're fighting there.
The war getting closer, and closer, and closer.
When they-- when the last minute, in that-- from Skarzysko, when they took us out, they made an appeal.
Everybody should be out from the barracks.
And they're going to send us out someplace else.
So there was-- there was-- there was one guy, an SS man, without one-- one arm, chopped off.
And one arm, he was going with a pistol.
And we were staying there.
And they were segregating.
Like, you left, you right, you left, you right.
They took out-- you know, they-- to send us away, to different camps.
OK, we're going to have to stop here and continue on with the story in tape three.
